metadata.title:
PRATT REVIEW


CLUB jOLTEO

POST GETS UP IN MEETING
AND DECLARES THE WHOLE
BUSINESS A PIECE OF HUM.
BUG.

pages.raw_text:
A severe Jolt from an unex-
pected quarter was received by
the Pratt-Review Nonpartisan club
last night at the meeting in the
conucil chamber. 1t was delivered
by Frank T. Post, who by invita-
ton appeared to address the club
on the proposed amendments to
the city charter, and by way of in
PRODUCTION told the audience that
its nonpartisan movement was a
humbug and piece of buncombe
and that he was republican and
would support his party ticket.


There were possibly 200 at the
meeting. The assemblage was
composed largely of lawyers.
preachers. bankers, brokers, mer-
chants and other gentlemen of po
lite occupations, who do not as a
rule earn their bread in the man.
ner enjoined by the scriptures, by
the sweat of the brow" The horny
handed sons of toll, who compose
the chief following of Mr.. ONO,
were noticeable by their absence,
as not to exceed a dozen working
men were present.


The prevailing note In the
speechmaking was a denunciation
of the undesirable elemnts' con-
stituting the cohorts of Ono" and
praising the high moral standard
of the gentlemen behind Mr.. Pratt,
not excepting of course those pres
ent. The first specimen Of the
ONO cohorts" to appear in evil
dence was Attorney Post, who
seemed only too glad to class him.
self with the "undesirables." Post
said he believed in nonpartisan
municipal politics but believed in
getting it in the right way, by the
adoption of an amendment to the
city charter.


Judge Nash filled the chair and
managed, by his felicitous re-
marks, to keep the interest alive
when ii seemed on the wane. w
A. Monten resigned as secretary
and Ernest E. Sargent, deputy
prosecuting attorney and secre
tary in the Moore campaign, was
